meta.name: Business Analysis Fundamentals (BABOK v3-aligned)
meta.description: Core business analysis concepts aligned with IIBA BABOK v3 - requirements, elicitation, modeling, strategy, stakeholders, and solution evaluation.

cards.definition: The practice of enabling change in an enterprise by defining needs and recommending solutions that deliver value.

Six core areas: Business Analysis Planning & Monitoring, Elicitation & Collaboration, Requirements Life Cycle Management, Strategy Analysis, Requirements Analysis & Design Definition, Solution Evaluation.
Defines how BA work will be performed, including stakeholders, deliverables, governance, and approach.
Activities to draw out information from stakeholders and confirm understanding.
Manages requirements from creation to retirement, ensuring alignment and traceability.
Defines business needs, assesses current state, and recommends future-state solutions.
Organizes, models, verifies, and validates requirements and designs.
Assesses solution performance and identifies improvements.
Anyone affected by the change or solution, including customers, users, sponsors, and SMEs.
Identifying stakeholders, their influence, needs, and communication preferences.
Common techniques include interviews, workshops, observation, surveys, prototyping, and document analysis.
Usable representations of a need, including business, stakeholder, solution, and transition requirements.
High-level goals and outcomes the organization wants to achieve.
Needs of stakeholders that must be met to achieve business requirements.
Capabilities and qualities the solution must have - functional and nonfunctional.
Capabilities needed to move from current state to future state.
Describe behaviors, features, and functions of the solution.
Describe solution qualities such as performance, security, usability, and reliability.
Ensures each requirement is linked to business objectives, stakeholders, and solution components.
Ranking requirements using methods like MoSCoW, Kano, or value-based scoring.
A description of how a user interacts with a system to achieve a goal.
A short requirement format used in agile: 'As a user, I want… so that…'.
Visual representation of workflows using BPMN or flowcharts.
Defines data structures, relationships, and rules using ERDs or data dictionaries.
Creating early visual representations of a solution to gather feedback.
Identifying the underlying cause of a problem using tools like the 5 Whys or fishbone diagrams.
Evaluates strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.
A justification for a proposed initiative, outlining benefits, costs, risks, and alternatives.
Understanding existing processes, capabilities, and problems.
Defining the desired outcomes, capabilities, and conditions after change.
Identifying differences between current and future states.
Defines the boundaries of the solution and what will be delivered.
Conditions that must be met for a requirement or deliverable to be accepted.
Ensuring requirements are defined correctly and meet quality standards.
Ensuring requirements support business goals and stakeholder needs.
cards.hint: Did we write the right thing?

cards.definition: Metrics used to evaluate whether a solution delivers expected value.
Quantifiable metrics used to evaluate performance against objectives.
Measures such as response time, uptime, scalability, and usability.
A plan describing how the organization will transition from current to future state.
Preparing the organization for solution deployment, including training and readiness.
Constraints or conditions that govern business behavior.
A list of key terms and definitions to ensure shared understanding.
Fictional representations of user types to guide design and requirements.
A visual technique for organizing user stories into workflows.
Ongoing process of reviewing, clarifying, and prioritizing backlog items.
A version of a product with just enough features to deliver value and gather feedback.
The sequence of activities that deliver value to a customer.
Understanding organizational goals, capabilities, and constraints to identify opportunities for improvement.
Selecting the best solution option based on feasibility, value, and constraints.
Key skills including analytical thinking, communication, problem-solving, and business knowledge.
A problem or opportunity that motivates the initiation of business analysis work.
A clear description of the issue to be solved, including impact and affected stakeholders.
Evaluating potential improvements or innovations that could deliver value.
A blueprint of the enterprise that aligns strategy, processes, capabilities, and information.
A visual representation of what the organization does, independent of how it is done.
cards.hint: What the business is capable of.

cards.definition: Shows the system boundary and interactions with external entities.
Describes how an entity changes state in response to events.
Defines business rules and logic using decision tables or decision trees.
Identifies interactions between systems, users, and external components.
Reviewing existing documentation to gather information about processes or systems.
Watching users perform tasks to understand workflows and challenges.
A collaborative session with stakeholders to gather requirements or solve problems.
A technique for generating ideas in a group setting.
A visual technique for organizing ideas around a central concept.
A tool for ranking requirements based on criteria such as value, cost, and risk.
A strategic template describing value proposition, customers, channels, and revenue streams.
The unique value a solution delivers to customers or stakeholders.
Comparing alternative solutions based on feasibility, cost, and value.
Evaluates technical, operational, and financial viability of a solution.
Compares expected benefits with estimated costs to justify investment.
Identifying and evaluating risks that may impact solution success.
Evaluating assumptions to determine their validity and impact.
A formal document outlining business needs and high-level requirements.
Describes how the solution will meet functional requirements.
Documents performance, security, usability, and other quality attributes.
A structured set of requirements tailored for a specific audience or purpose.
A formal evaluation of requirements for clarity, completeness, and feasibility.
A table linking requirements to objectives, designs, tests, and stakeholders.
The difference between expected and actual solution performance.
Assessing how easy and intuitive a solution is for end users.
Deploying a solution to a small group to validate performance before full rollout.
Comparing two solution variations to determine which performs better.
Limitations such as budget, technology, culture, or regulations.
Radical redesign of processes to achieve major performance improvements.
Small, continuous enhancements to existing processes.
Comparing performance against industry standards or competitors.
Visualizing the customer's experience across touchpoints.
A diagram showing interactions between customers, frontstage, and backstage processes.
A trigger that initiates a business process or requirement.
Validating that the solution meets business needs and acceptance criteria.
Evaluating whether the organization is prepared for solution deployment.
cards.hint: Are we ready to go live?

cards.definition: Assessing how changes affect processes, systems, and stakeholders.
cards.hint: Who and what will be affected?

cards.definition: Determining whether people, processes, and technology can support the change.
Identifying skills gaps and training required for solution adoption.
Acts as a liaison between stakeholders and solution teams to ensure value delivery.
Ability to break down complex problems and evaluate information objectively.
Understanding how components interact within a larger environment.
Guiding discussions to ensure productive outcomes and stakeholder alignment.
Reaching agreement among stakeholders with differing priorities.
Understanding how organizations operate, generate value, and make decisions.
A measurable result that the organization expects from a change initiative.
Common groups include customers, end users, sponsors, SMEs, regulators, and suppliers.
Defines how and when stakeholders will be involved throughout the BA lifecycle.
Identifying objectives, selecting techniques, and scheduling sessions before elicitation begins.
Validating that information gathered is accurate, complete, and understood.
Interactive elicitation techniques such as card sorting or product box.
A visual technique showing the sequence of user interactions with a system.
Shows interactions between system components over time.
Represents system objects, attributes, and relationships.
A repository of data elements, definitions, and formats.
Ensuring consistent terminology across stakeholders and documents.
Identifying and defining rules that govern business behavior.
Common categories include performance, security, usability, reliability, and maintainability.
Metadata such as priority, status, owner, and complexity.
Defines how requirements are approved, managed, and communicated.
A structured method for evaluating and approving requirement changes.
Evaluating how a proposed change affects scope, stakeholders, and solution components.
Uncontrolled expansion of solution features without approval.
Evaluating the expected value delivered by a solution option.
Measures such as ROI, customer satisfaction, cycle time, or cost reduction.
Potential issues that may reduce solution performance or value.
Limitations such as technology, budget, or regulatory requirements.
Conditions that must be met for the solution to be accepted by stakeholders.
Ensuring the solution meets business needs and delivers expected value.
Tracking solution performance after deployment.
Supports the team by refining backlog items, clarifying requirements, and ensuring value delivery.
A prioritized list of work items representing requirements and enhancements.
A large body of work that can be broken down into multiple user stories.
A service or capability that delivers value to users.
Conditions that define when a user story is complete.
Relative sizing techniques such as story points or t-shirt sizing.
A high-level timeline showing planned releases and features.
Identifying improvements after each iteration.
Evaluates organizational strengths and weaknesses related to change.
Examining workflows to identify inefficiencies and improvement opportunities.
Breaking down processes into smaller, manageable components.
Visualizing the flow of value through a process to identify waste.
Identifying what customers truly need versus what they request.
Evaluating market trends, competitors, and customer segments.
Identifying laws and regulations that impact the solution.
Evaluating limitations such as budget, technology, or resources.
Risks associated with moving from current state to future state.
Preparing people and processes for adopting new solutions.
Defines how users will be trained to adopt the new solution.
Defines how information will be shared with stakeholders throughout the project.
Methods such as round-robin, parking lot, and silent brainstorming.
Techniques for resolving stakeholder disagreements, such as compromise or collaboration.
Ability to persuade stakeholders without formal authority.
Fully focusing on the speaker to understand their message and intent.
Acting with integrity, transparency, and respect in all BA activities.
High-level objectives that guide strategic decision-making and influence solution direction.
cards.hint: What the organization aims to achieve.

cards.definition: Forces that motivate organizational change, such as competition, regulation, or technology shifts.
cards.hint: What pushes the business to change.

cards.definition: Questions that help determine what performance metrics are needed.
Requirements that are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
Events that prompt the need for elicitation, such as new regulations or system failures.
Observing users in their natural work environment to understand tasks and challenges.
Analyzing an existing system to understand its components and behavior.
cards.hint: Understand the system by taking it apart.

cards.definition: Ranking gaps based on impact, urgency, and feasibility.
A missing or weak capability that prevents achieving business goals.
cards.hint: What the business cannot do yet.

cards.definition: Limitations such as budget, culture, technology, or regulations that affect solution options.
cards.hint: Boundaries the business must work within.

cards.definition: The environment in which the solution operates, including systems, users, and constraints.
The area of the organization affected by the solution, including processes and stakeholders.
cards.hint: The part of the business involved.

cards.definition: Rules and calculations that determine how a system behaves.
A structured representation of business rules using conditions and outcomes.
A branching diagram that models decisions and their possible consequences.
Exploring different possible future states to understand impacts and risks.
Identifying events that trigger business processes or system actions.
A point in a process where flow is restricted, causing delays.
A process diagram that shows responsibilities across roles or departments.
A combination of interviewing and observation performed in the user's environment.
Assessing usability based on established design principles.
Understanding organizational culture to determine readiness for change.
Visualizing stakeholder power, interest, and relationships.
A fictional profile representing a stakeholder type to guide analysis.
When stakeholders have competing or incompatible requirements.
A requirement that can be interpreted in multiple ways.
Breaking high-level requirements into smaller, more detailed ones.
A list of criteria to ensure requirement quality.
Includes walkthroughs, prototyping, and acceptance testing.
An assumption about how a proposed solution will deliver value.
Testing solution ideas quickly through prototypes or pilots.
Loss of expected value due to poor adoption, defects, or misalignment.
Ensuring expected benefits are achieved after solution deployment.
The degree to which the organization is prepared for the new solution.
The point at which an organization cannot absorb additional change.
Stakeholder reluctance to adopt new processes or solutions.
Obstacles such as jargon, assumptions, or cultural differences that hinder understanding.
Using open-ended and probing questions to uncover deeper insights.
Helping stakeholders reach consensus using structured techniques.
Reducing tension through empathy, reframing, and neutral language.
Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement - fallback option if negotiation fails.
Recognizing and managing emotions to improve stakeholder relationships.
Using quantitative techniques to evaluate options or performance.
Representing key concepts and relationships within a business domain.
A narrative describing how users interact with a solution in a real-world context.
cards.hint: Story of how the solution is used.

cards.definition: A timeline showing planned releases, capabilities, and dependencies.
Risks that affect the organization's ability to achieve strategic goals.
Ensuring solution metrics support organizational KPIs.
cards.hint: Align solution metrics to business goals.

cards.definition: The ability of a solution to handle increased demand or growth.
cards.hint: Can it grow with the business?

cards.definition: Maximizing the benefits delivered by a solution while minimizing waste.
The measurable benefit an organization gains from a solution, including financial and non-financial outcomes.
Ensuring proposed solutions support organizational goals and direction.
Analyzing external factors such as market trends, competitors, and regulations.
Evaluating the organization's strengths, weaknesses, and resources.
cards.hint: Assess what the business can do.

cards.definition: A framework that links goals, objectives, strategies, and tactics.
A strategic tool measuring performance across financial, customer, process, and learning perspectives.
A long-term plan showing major initiatives, dependencies, and timelines.
Creating narratives that illustrate how users interact with a future solution.
A collaborative modeling technique to identify domain events and workflows.
Representing key business concepts and their relationships.
Understanding how a solution fits within its environment and interacts with external systems.
Testing process models using scenarios to predict performance.
Shows how data moves through a system, including inputs, outputs, and storage.
Maps which processes Create, Read, Update, or Delete data entities.
A relationship where one requirement relies on another.
cards.hint: Requirements that rely on each other.

cards.definition: The likelihood that a requirement will remain unchanged.
The frequency and extent of requirement changes.
Leveraging existing requirements for new projects or solutions.
A centralized location for storing and managing requirements.
The approved version of requirements used for development and change control.
Factors used to evaluate solution options, such as cost, value, and risk.
A technique that assigns weights to criteria and scores options.
The economic impact of delaying a feature or solution.
The value of the best alternative not chosen.
Identifying creative opportunities for new products or improvements.
A human-centered approach to problem-solving emphasizing empathy and experimentation.
Understanding user emotions, thoughts, and behaviors.
Using data to analyze customer interactions across touchpoints.
A formal agreement defining service performance expectations.
Identifying and categorizing defects to improve solution quality.
Measures such as usage rates, satisfaction, and training completion.
cards.hint: How well users adopt the solution.

cards.definition: The level of sophistication in processes, culture, and capabilities.
Signs that the organization is prepared for change, such as leadership support and resource availability.
Activities that sustain new behaviors after implementation.
Assessing whether training improved user capability and adoption.
A measure of how well stakeholder expectations are met.
The degree to which stakeholders participate and support the initiative.
A structured plan outlining topics, timing, and activities for a workshop.
Remaining unbiased while guiding discussions.
The interactions and behaviors within a group that affect collaboration.
The emotional quality of communication, such as formal, friendly, or assertive.
Choosing the most effective channel for a message, such as email, meeting, or chat.
Recognizing biases that affect decision-making, such as anchoring or confirmation bias.
Evaluating information objectively to form reasoned judgments.
Using innovative thinking to generate new solutions.
Limiting the time spent on an activity to improve focus and efficiency.
Delivering value incrementally through frequent releases.
Criteria that a backlog item must meet before being accepted into a sprint.
A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete.
Ranking work items based on expected value, cost, and risk.
A long-term aspirational description of what the organization aims to become.
Defines the organization's purpose and core reason for existence.
High-level focus areas that guide organizational initiatives.
A prediction of how a proposed solution will impact business outcomes.
The level of risk an organization is willing to accept.
cards.hint: How much risk the business tolerates.

cards.definition: A strategic analysis of strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.
The network of partners, competitors, regulators, and stakeholders influencing the organization.
cards.hint: The environment the business operates in.

cards.definition: Examining primary and support activities that create value.
A measure of how well a capability performs relative to best practices.
Ensuring solutions fit within the organization's architecture standards.
A framework for assessing the maturity of business processes.
A visual tool highlighting process performance, risks, or issues.
A modeling technique showing events, functions, and logical relationships.
Defines what is inside and outside the system being analyzed.
The structure and organization of information within a system.
Tracking data origins, transformations, and destinations.
cards.hint: Where data comes from and goes.

cards.definition: Common pitfalls such as leading questions or assuming stakeholder knowledge.
A technique where participants generate ideas individually before sharing.
Each participant contributes ideas in turn to ensure equal participation.
Reviewing requirements for unclear or subjective language.
Ensuring requirements include all necessary details and conditions.
Ensuring requirements do not conflict with each other.
Evaluating whether requirements can realistically be implemented.
Determining whether a requirement can be verified through testing.
Resolving conflicts and reaching agreement on requirement priorities.
Formal approval of requirements by stakeholders.
The initial performance metrics used to compare future results.
Changes in performance metrics over time.
The minimum acceptable level of performance.
Comparing actual performance to expected results to identify gaps.
Evaluating whether the solution can handle increased demand.
How easily a solution can be updated or repaired.
The long-term viability of a solution in terms of cost, support, and adaptability.
A high-level description of the product's purpose and value.
A long-term objective for the product that guides backlog prioritization.
A roadmap showing planned releases and major features.
The flow of value from idea to delivery in an agile environment.
Measures such as cycle time, lead time, and throughput.
The cost of rework caused by choosing quick solutions instead of long-term ones.
A timeboxed research activity to reduce uncertainty.
Breaking large user stories into smaller, deliverable pieces.
Validating that a story meets acceptance criteria.
Agreements that guide behavior during workshops.
A technique for capturing off-topic items without derailing discussion.
Ensuring discussions stay within allocated time.
Presenting information in a way that influences understanding and perception.
Anything that interferes with message clarity, such as jargon or assumptions.
The mental effort required to process information.
cards.hint: How hard it is to understand.

cards.definition: Representing system components, interactions, and boundaries.
A plan showing when and how expected value will be delivered and measured.
